overview: The HTML Filter processes HTML and XHTML documents for localization. It supports both well-formed XHTML and non-conforming HTML via two built-in configurations: `okf_html` (default, lenient parsing) and `okf_html-wellFormed` (strict, enables structural groupings like tables and lists). Configuration is written in YAML and maps HTML elements and attributes to extraction rules that control how the filter treats each tag.

parser.assumeWellformed.help:
Uses an XML-based parser for faster processing. All structural tags that map to `GROUP` rules **must** have matching start and end tags ‚Äî the filter will error on unclosed tags.

The default (`okf_html`) configuration uses lenient parsing; the `okf_html-wellFormed` configuration enables this.
notes: Any structural tags mapped to `GROUP` rules must be well-formed (have both start and end tags), otherwise the filter will return an error.

parser.preserveWhitespace.help:
Retains the original whitespace in extracted text units. Without this, whitespace is normalized.

Individual elements can also preserve whitespace via the `PRESERVE_WHITESPACE` rule type (e.g., `<pre>` tags) ‚Äî this is a global override.

elements.help:
Maps HTML element names to extraction rule configurations. Each key is a **lowercase** element name, and the value defines how the filter processes that element. Each element rule can include: - **`ruleTypes`** ‚Äî how the filter treats the tag (see below) - **`translatableAttributes`** ‚Äî attributes to extract for translation - **`idAttributes`** ‚Äî attributes providing the segment ID - **`conditions`** ‚Äî restrict the rule to elements matching attribute conditions - **`elementType`** ‚Äî corresponding XLIFF 1.2 `type` value **Rule types:** - `INLINE` ‚Äî tag inside a text run (``, ``, ``) - `GROUP` ‚Äî structural grouping (``, `
`) ‚Äî **must be well-formed** if parser is in well-formed mode - `TEXTUNIT` ‚Äî starts a text unit (`

`, ``, `<h1>`) - `EXCLUDE` ‚Äî suppresses extraction until end tag - `INCLUDE` ‚Äî overrides exclusion for child elements - `PRESERVE_WHITESPACE` ‚Äî preserves whitespace as-is (`<pre>`) - `ATTRIBUTES_ONLY` ‚Äî only attributes are localizable, not content **Conditions** use the syntax `[attribute, operation, value]` where operation is `EQUALS`, `NOT_EQUALS`, or `MATCHES` (regex).
notes: All element names must be **lowercase** in the configuration, regardless of casing in the document.
Elements with a prefix must be declared with the prefix in single quotes (e.g., `'xml:lang'`).
examples:
With translatable attributes:
```yaml
area:
  ruleTypes: [ATTRIBUTES_ONLY]
  translatableAttributes: [accesskey, area, alt]
```

Conditional translatable attributes:
```yaml
input:
  ruleTypes: [INLINE]
  translatableAttributes:
    alt: [type, NOT_EQUALS, [file, hidden, image, password]]
    value: [type, NOT_EQUALS, [file, hidden, image, password]]
```

attributes.help:
Maps HTML attribute names to global extraction rules. These apply across all elements unless overridden by element-specific `translatableAttributes`.

Attribute rule types:
- `ATTRIBUTE_TRANS` ‚Äî translatable attribute
- `ATTRIBUTE_WRITABLE` ‚Äî writable/modifiable but not translatable
- `ATTRIBUTE_READONLY` ‚Äî extracted but cannot be modified
notes: All attribute names must be **lowercase** in the configuration, regardless of casing in the document.
Attributes with a prefix must be declared with the prefix in single quotes (e.g., `'xml:lang'`).

inlineCodes.help:
Define regular expressions to capture spans of extracted text that should be treated as inline codes. Useful for protecting variables or placeholders from modification.

The rules use the Okapi code finder `#v1` format. Backslashes must be escaped in standard YAML notation, or use YAML block scalar (`|-`) syntax to avoid double-escaping.
examples:
Protect `VAR1`, `VAR2`, etc.:
```yaml
useCodeFinder: true
codeFinderRules: |-
  #v1
  count.i=1
  rule0=\bVAR\d\b
```

Input `

Number of files = VAR1

` ‚Üí `VAR1` becomes an inline code

skipEncodingDeclaration.help:
Controls whether the filter adds an encoding declaration `<meta>` tag to the output when the input has none.

By default, if the input has no declared encoding, the filter adds a `<meta>` tag (or `<meta />` for XHTML) ‚Äî but only if a `<head>` element exists in the file.

processingNotes: If the document has an encoding declaration, it is used; otherwise the default encoding specified in filter options is used.
For UTF-8 output: a BOM is written only if the input was also UTF-8 **and** had a BOM. Transcoding from non-UTF-8 to UTF-8 never adds a BOM.
Output line-break type matches the original input.
Character and numeric entities are converted to Unicode. DTD/schema-defined entities pass through unchanged.
By default the filter does **not** create structural groupings (tables, lists, etc.). Use `okf_html-wellFormed` for structural groups.
The filter attempts to clean up common HTML syntax errors (e.g., unquoted attributes) by default during post-processing.
limitations: Content of `<style>` and `<script>` elements is not extracted.
Server-side script tags (PHP, ASPX, JSP, etc.) are not formally supported and are treated as non-translatable.

examples.title: Excluding all content by default
examples.description: Use `exclude_by_default` to suppress all extraction, then selectively include only specific elements. This produces a more concise configuration when you only need a few elements translated.

examples.title: Inline CDATA handling
examples.description: When CDATA sections appear inline within text and break the flow, enable `inlineCdata` to treat `<![CDATA[` and `]]>` as inline tags.
examples.output: Extracted as a single text unit with CDATA markers as inline codes.

examples.title: Character entity references in output
examples.description: Use `escapeCharacters` to force specific extended characters to be output as HTML character entity references instead of raw Unicode.

examples.title: Quote mode configuration
examples.description: Control how quote and apostrophe characters are escaped in output. Mode 3 (double quotes only) is commonly used to avoid unnecessary escaping of apostrophes in natural language text.
